content:
Currently our workflow is that commits are added to GitHub, and then we create syncing series to synchronize them to pin-init-next. But these series typically got no reviews at all, and leaving it on the list untaken reduces testing time in linux-next.

I wonder if the workflow can be changed similar to how stable tree is managed, where the patch is applied directly to pin-init-next, and then a email of "patch xxx is added to pin-init-next" is sent to the list? If something is broken then it could be taken out still or fixed by a follow-up commit.

For bigger features or things that touch files outside pin-init we can still fallback to the syncing series workflow.

Thoughts @Miguel Ojeda @Benno Lossin ?

So the standard rules are that the patches need to have been posted to a mailing list etc. There are times that I also feel the same, i.e. that it would save me time to just apply some cleanups directly, but I still go through the list to be "proper" and to give a chance to people to give tags etc.

Regarding time in linux-next, if they are small series, then I don't think it matters much, and you could always put them tentatively in linux-next and rebase later if needed.

i.e. that would give you the best of both worlds: standard process, and yet good testing times.

But if it would simplify your life significantly, perhaps Mark is willing to make an exception for you since it is a vendored thing and you are the upstream maintainer (unlike e.g. zerocopy). But we would need to then explain it to Linus too and so on. Not sure if it is worth it, and Mark may fear others may ask for similar exceptions as well and it snowballs...
Is there a rule on how long a patch should be on the list? Because the "patch added notification" is basically "send to list and immediately apply".
Basically I just want to apply and forget, but obviously if someone replied with some issues then it could always be taken out

I think 1 or 2 weeks is a good time for features. But for mechanical changes that no one is giving you reviews, you could always simply apply them earlier saying "Tags still welcome for a week" or similar, as I sometimes do.

i.e. in my view, the important bit is to give people still the chance to review.
If no one sends you a review, then you get the linux-next time and nobody can complain about the process wasn't followed, and you avoid having to ask for exceptions.
(By mechanical I mean that it comes from upstream, I don't mean that the work you are doing is trivial, of course)
And if you do get a review tag, then well, the hash will not be the same, but the time in linux-next was still useful.

At one point I had this staging workflow idea (discussed in #pin-init > staging workflow), but I haven't come around and fully implemented it. Most things are implemented in my staging-workflow-playground repo, except for the github app that allows us to integrate the bot with the rulesets needed to have multiple contributers.
An alternative could also be to just have some custom scripts that handle all of that locally
We could send out the patch series when we apply it to the staging branch

at which point we can still rebase, add tags and all of the other stuff. Then at some point we create a new staging branch and the current one becomes frozen. That frozen branch then becomes pin-init-next and contains all changes for the cycle
Why do you need the staging branch and not directly apply it to pin-init-next?
to apply any mailing list suggestions and reviewed-by trailers

So what I understood from Gary is that there are virtually never review comments, i.e. please see the discussion above.

Otherwise, if you do expect reviews, then yes, of course, please avoid to put them directly in linux-next (which is better, since it is the standard procedure anyway, but we were discussing how to save you time/complexity).
